one_line_takeaway: Grounding a zero-shot LLM (never trained on real patient data) in DSM-5/ICD-10 knowledge via RAG produces psychiatric questionnaire synthetic data that is competitive with CTGAN/TVAE on pairwise symptom structure and wins outright on two of six anxiety disorders, while also exposing that average k-map linkage risk is an unreliable privacy metric for small categorical domains since TVAE scores best on it despite near-total record duplication.
key_finding: On fidelity (Table I), CTGAN generally achieves the lowest JSD and ED2 across all six disorders (e.g., Social Anxiety JSD 0.021 vs. the LLM's best of 0.088; Agoraphobia ED2 0.033 vs. LLM's 0.097), while TVAE attains the lowest MAE_V in Agoraphobia (0.159) and Generalized Anxiety (0.144). The knowledge-augmented LLM is competitive on pairwise (MAE_V) structure and attains the best MAE_V of all methods, including CTGAN/TVAE, in two disorders: Separation Anxiety (DUAL-KB, MAE_V=0.148 [0.105, 0.193]) and Social Anxiety (DUAL-KB, MAE_V=0.163 [0.127, 0.199]). Within LLM variants, all knowledge-augmented configurations (DSM-V, ICD-10, DUAL-KB) outperform NO-KB and random generation across metrics and disorders in the paper's own comparison. The ablation (Table II) shows mostly positive deltas over NO-KB with several CIs excluding zero -- e.g., for Social Anxiety, DUAL-KB yields delta-JSD=0.026 [0.011,0.043], delta-MAE_V=0.067 [0.041,0.096], delta-ED2=0.029 [0.008,0.052]; Separation Anxiety shows a significant ED2 reduction under ICD-10 (delta=0.035 [0.019,0.053]) -- supporting the claim that clinical retrieval improves fidelity, though not uniformly across all disorder/metric combinations. On privacy (Table III), the LLM variants show elevated Exact Overlap (roughly 0.06-0.30) and average Risk_k-map in a narrow band of about 0.017-0.032, which the authors frame as combinatorial coincidence in a small discrete answer space rather than memorization, since the LLM never saw real records. TVAE is flagged as a practical privacy failure (Exact Overlap up to about 0.879, near-match Share up to about 1.00) despite paradoxically registering the lowest average Risk_k-map (about 0.007) in every disorder -- the paper's headline privacy-metric-artifact finding.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is a zero-shot, knowledge-guided synthetic data generator for psychiatric tabular questionnaire data: a Mistral-7B LLM is prompted (via a conversational per-item loop, sampled patient demographics/latent response traits, and RAG-retrieved clinical knowledge snippets from DSM-5 and/or ICD-10) to role-play a patient completing a DSM-5 severity self-report, with a schema-constrained parser extracting a discrete 0-4 Likert score per item. Critically, the generator is never trained or fine-tuned on real patient data at any stage -- retrieval only supplies definitional/criteria text, not patient records -- which the authors position as a structurally different route to privacy (privacy-by-design via data independence) than the noise-injection route used by CTGAN/TVAE or DP-based generators. They benchmark four LLM knowledge-base configurations (NO-KB, DSM-V, ICD-10, DUAL-KB) against CTGAN, TVAE, and a random baseline on six anxiety-disorder tables derived from a real 564-participant OSF dataset, evaluating both statistical fidelity (JSD, bias-corrected Cramer's V error MAE_V, squared energy distance ED2) and privacy proxies (exact overlap, 5th-percentile nearest-neighbor Hamming distance, near-match share, average k-map linkage risk).
threat_model: This is not an adversarial-attack paper: the authors explicitly state as a limitation that they 'assessed privacy only with proxy metrics' and 'did not perform adversarial attacks such as membership inference.' The privacy concern addressed is data-dependency risk in conventional generators (CTGAN/TVAE and DP-based methods train on real patient records and can therefore memorize or leak identifiable information), quantified indirectly via exact-overlap and linkage-risk statistics rather than via an attacker model with defined capabilities. The paper's central empirical finding on this front is a metric-artifact critique of the k-map linkage-risk measure itself: TVAE shows extensive record-level duplication (Exact Overlap ranging from about 0.415 up to about 0.879, and near-match Share from about 0.874 up to about 1.000 across the six disorders) yet still achieves the lowest average Risk_k-map (about 0.007-0.008) in every disorder table, because duplicating records that fall into large quasi-identifier equivalence classes mechanically shrinks the 1/k(q) average even as literal copying occurs at the row level. The authors argue this shows exact-overlap and near-match metrics should be prioritized over average k-map risk for small-domain categorical tabular data -- a methodological/metric-validity finding about privacy evaluation, not a demonstrated attack or defense.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: privacy was assessed only via proxy metrics (exact overlap, near-match, k-map linkage risk); no adversarial attacks such as membership inference were performed. The six disorders were modeled independently with no comorbidity or longitudinal modeling, a significant simplification since comorbidity is described as 'the rule rather than the exception' in psychiatric practice. Several fidelity-ablation deltas have wide confidence intervals that include zero, so improvements are disorder- and metric-dependent rather than uniform. The short, discrete (5-point Likert) item scales may inflate proximity/overlap measures and may not generalize to richer or continuous feature spaces. Post-selection retraining of CTGAN/TVAE on the full dataset was done without re-applying the privacy gates used during tuning, so reported final overlaps may exceed the tuning-time thresholds. Only one LLM backbone (Mistral-7B) and one real dataset (OSF, N=564) were tested.

datasets_used: OSF open dataset (Vidal Arenas et al., DOI 10.17605/OSF.IO/JZ4GE): DSM-5 Severity Measure responses from 564 adult participants, split into six disorder-specific tables (Separation Anxiety, Specific Phobia, Social Anxiety, Panic, Agoraphobia, Generalized Anxiety)
quantitative_results: Fidelity best MAE_V for LLM (DUAL-KB) vs. all methods: Separation Anxiety 0.148 [0.105,0.193]; Social Anxiety 0.163 [0.127,0.199]
CTGAN lowest JSD/ED2 across all six disorders, e.g. Social Anxiety JSD=0.021 [0.016,0.026], ED2=0.013 [0.010,0.018]
TVAE lowest MAE_V in Agoraphobia (0.159 [0.129,0.192]) and Generalized Anxiety (0.144 [0.118,0.178])
Ablation delta over NO-KB, Social Anxiety DUAL-KB: delta-JSD=0.026 [0.011,0.043], delta-MAE_V=0.067 [0.041,0.096], delta-ED2=0.029 [0.008,0.052]
Ablation delta over NO-KB, Separation Anxiety ICD-10: delta-ED2=0.035 [0.019,0.053]
Privacy Table III: TVAE Exact Overlap ranges ~0.415-0.879 and near-match Share ~0.874-1.000 across disorders, with average Risk_k-map lowest of all methods (~0.007-0.008) in every disorder
Privacy Table III: CTGAN Exact Overlap ~0.000-0.011, near-match Share ~0.011-0.103, average Risk_k-map ~0.018-0.058
Privacy Table III: LLM variants (NO-KB/DSM-V/ICD-10/DUAL-KB) Exact Overlap roughly 0.06-0.30 and average Risk_k-map in a narrow band ~0.017-0.032
baselines_compared: CTGAN (Conditional Tabular GAN, trained on real data via SDV library)
TVAE (tabular VAE, trained on real data via SDV library)
Random baseline (marginals sampled empirically for sex/age, items sampled uniformly)
NO-KB LLM variant (Mistral-7B with no retrieval, used as the ablation reference point)
